title: The Governance Inversion Hypothesis: Why More AI Regulation May Produce Less Organisational Control
abstract: arXiv:2606.26117v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: This paper introduces the Governance Inversion Hypothesis (GIH) to explain a growing paradox in artificial intelligence (AI) governance: under conditions of increasing regulatory expansion and technological complexity, organisations may become more formally governed while simultaneously experiencing a decline in operational control over AI systems. Existing AI governance frameworks generally assume that stronger regulation improves accountability, oversight, and organisational control. This paper challenges that assumption by arguing that governance formalisation itself may contribute to the erosion of control in AI-intensive environments. Drawing on institutional theory, organisational governance research, accountability scholarship, and emerging AI governance literature, the paper develops a conceptual framework explaining how regulatory expansion may weaken operational authority through four interconnected mechanisms: authority frag

Abstract: arXiv:2606.26117v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: This paper introduces the Governance Inversion Hypothesis (GIH) to explain a growing paradox in artificial intelligence (AI) governance: under conditions of increasing regulatory expansion and technological complexity, organisations may become more formally governed while simultaneously experiencing a decline in operational control over AI systems. translated_title: 治理倒置假说：为何更多AI监管可能导致更少的组织控制
translated_abstract: arXiv:2606.26117v1 公告类型：交叉 摘要：本文提出了治理倒置假说（GIH），以解释人工智能（AI）治理中日益加剧的一个悖论：在监管扩张和技术复杂性不断增加的条件下，组织可能在形式上受到更严格的治理，同时却经历对AI系统操作控制权的下降。现有的AI治理框架通常假设更强的监管能改善问责、监督和组织控制。本文挑战了这一假设，认为治理形式化本身可能助长AI密集型环境中控制权的侵蚀。借鉴制度理论、组织治理研究、问责制学术成果以及新兴的AI治理文献，本文构建了一个概念框架，解释了监管扩张如何通过四种相互关联的机制削弱操作权威：权威碎片化。
tldr: 本文提出治理倒置假说，指出在AI治理中，监管扩张可能通过权威碎片化等机制，导致组织形式上的治理增强而实际控制权下降。
en_tdlr: This paper proposes the Governance Inversion Hypothesis, arguing that in AI governance, regulatory expansion may lead to increased formal governance but decreased operational control through mechanisms such as authority fragmentation.
